g.data-narration: Welcome to Polynomial Regression! Today we will explore how to model curved relationships in data using this powerful extension of linear regression.
Polynomial regression models non-linear relationships by adding powers of features. It captures curved patterns that straight lines cannot represent.
g.text: Extension of linear regression that models non-linear relationships using
polynomial terms: y = w0 + w1x + w2x2 + w3x3 + ... + wnxn
Still "linear in parameters" uses OLS for estimation!

g.data-narration: Real-world data often has curved patterns. A straight line fails to capture these, but polynomial curves flex to fit the underlying relationship.
The polynomial regression equation extends linear regression by including higher-order terms. Each coefficient multiplies its corresponding power of x.
g.text: Y = b0 + b1X + b2X2 + b3X3 + ... + bnXn + epsilon

g.data-narration: Different polynomial degrees create different curve shapes. Degree 1 is a line, degree 2 creates parabolas, and degree 3 creates S-curves.
Despite modeling curves, polynomial regression is linear in parameters. This means we can use standard OLS estimation because coefficients combine additively.
g.text: Linear COMBINATION of coefficients - NOT linear in features

g.data-narration: Implementation uses feature transformation: create new columns X squared, X cubed, and so on, then apply standard linear regression on the expanded matrix.
Underfitting occurs with low polynomial degrees. The model is too simple to capture non-linear patterns, resulting in high error on both training and test data.
g.text: - Model is too simple for the data

g.data-narration: Overfitting happens with high degrees. The model passes through every training point but oscillates wildly, capturing noise rather than true patterns.
g.text: - Captures NOISE, not the true pattern
Oscillation at boundaries with high degree

g.data-narration: The optimal degree balances bias and variance. Use cross-validation to find the degree with lowest validation error, or plot error vs degree and find the elbow.
g.text: Find the elbow where validation error is lowest

g.data-narration: Feature scaling is critical for polynomial regression. Higher powers amplify magnitude differences dramatically, causing numerical instability without normalization.
g.text: StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler before polynomial features

g.data-narration: Regularization prevents overfitting by penalizing large coefficients. Ridge (L2) shrinks all coefficients, while Lasso (L1) can eliminate unnecessary terms.
g.text: Degree 3 or higher always benefits from regularization

g.data-narration: The bias-variance tradeoff explains model behavior across degrees. Low degrees have high bias but low variance, while high degrees have low bias but high variance.
To summarize: polynomial regression extends linear regression to capture curved relationships while still using OLS estimation. Choose degree carefully using cross-validation.
g.text: - Polynomial regression models non-linear relationships using polynomial terms
- Still linear in parameters - uses OLS estimation
- Choose degree via cross-validation to balance bias-variance
- ALWAYS scale features before creating polynomial terms

g.data-narration: For exams: remember it is linear in parameters but non-linear in features. Know how degree affects bias/variance, and always scale polynomial features!
g.text: - It is LINEAR in parameters, NON-LINEAR in features
